text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Gastrointestinal Oncology Service

ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending Physician]
FELLOW: [GI Oncology Fellow]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Okafor is a 58-year-old Nigerian-American male with Stage IV adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, bilobar hepatic metastases) presenting for routine follow-up during maintenance systemic therapy, approximately 5 weeks after resuming 5-fluorouracil/leucovorin plus bevacizumab following recovery from laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Okafor was initially diagnosed with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma approximately nine months ago with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases at presentation. His molecular profile was notable for KRAS G12D mutation, BRAF V600E wild-type, NRAS wild-type, MSS by both IHC and NGS, with additional alterations including TP53 R248W, APC R1450*, SMAD4 R361H, PIK3CA E545K, and PTEN loss. CEA at diagnosis was 187.3 ng/mL, CA 19-9 was 412 U/mL. Given the KRAS-mutant status, anti-EGFR therapy was not an option and the patient proceeded with first-line FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab.

He completed 8 cycles of FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab (with oxaliplatin dose reduced to 75 mg/m2 after cycle 3 for grade 2 neuropathy superimposed on underlying diabetic peripheral neuropathy), at which point oxaliplatin was discontinued altogether after cycle 8 due to progression to grade 3 sensory neuropathy. He subsequently completed cycles 9 through 12 with 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab (de-escalated maintenance backbone). Restaging at cycle 12 demonstrated sustained partial response -- hepatic lesions were reduced compared to baseline (dominant segment VII lesion down from 3.2 cm to 1.8 cm), and the sigmoid primary was stable at 2.8 cm.

Following multidisciplinary discussion at that time, colorectal surgery proceeded with laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy approximately seven months after initial diagnosis. The procedure was uncomplicated with final pathology showing pT3 N2a disease (3 of 18 nodes positive), margins clear, without significant pathologic treatment response. Bevacizumab was appropriately held for four weeks perioperatively.

Approximately eight months after initial diagnosis, once surgical recovery was confirmed and wound healing was complete, Mr. Okafor resumed maintenance systemic therapy with 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab at standard doses (5-FU 2400 mg/m2 continuous infusion over 46 hours every 14 days, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 day 1, bevacizumab 5 mg/kg day 1 every 14 days). CEA at resumption was 38.4 ng/mL. He has now completed approximately 3 cycles of maintenance therapy since surgery and presents today for routine follow-up.

He reports tolerating the current regimen reasonably well. His primary ongoing symptoms are:
- Peripheral neuropathy: He describes numbness and tingling predominantly in the bilateral feet and, to a lesser extent, the hands. Since oxaliplatin was stopped and gabapentin was added, he reports a modest improvement from the severe grade 3 functional impairment he experienced at the peak of oxaliplatin toxicity. He is able to perform most activities of daily living, though buttoning shirts and other fine motor tasks remain moderately challenging. He denies falls or significant balance difficulties on flat ground, though he is cautious on uneven terrain.
- Fatigue: Mild, grade 1. He is sleeping adequately and managing light activities including working part-time in an administrative capacity at his school district.
- Nausea: Minimal, not requiring antiemetic therapy between infusion days.
- Diarrhea: Denies. Bowel function has been largely normal since colectomy, with 2-3 formed stools per day, which represents his new baseline.
- Appetite: Good. He has maintained his weight. No significant weight loss since the surgical period.
- Dyspnea: Denies shortness of breath at rest or with ordinary daily activity.
- Blood pressure: He reports checking at home; most readings in the 130s-140s/80s-90s range. He has been adherent to his antihypertensive medications.
- Neuropathic pain: Managed with gabapentin 300 mg three times daily; he states this provides roughly 30-40% relief. He was referred to neurology and has had one visit; they are following.

Denies chest pain, hemoptysis, hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, jaundice, abdominal pain, or change in stool caliber. He denies fever, chills, or night sweats. No new bone pain or headaches.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Mild fatigue (grade 1). Weight stable. No unintentional weight loss. No fevers.
- HEENT: No oral mucositis. No dysgeusia beyond mild persistence from prior 5-FU cycles.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain. No palpitations. Home blood pressure readings consistent with improved but not fully normalized hypertension.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea, no cough, no hemoptysis.
- GI: Bowel function near baseline post-colectomy. No abdominal pain. No nausea/vomiting. No diarrhea. No bloating or obstruction symptoms.
- Hepatic: No jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, or scleral icterus.
- Genitourinary: No dysuria. No hematuria. Urinary function normal.
- Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain. Peripheral neuropathy in feet and hands as described (grade 2, improved from grade 3).
- Neurological: Neuropathy as above. No headaches. No focal neurologic deficits. No cognitive changes.
- Dermatologic: No hand-foot syndrome. No rash. Port site without erythema or tenderness.
- Endocrine/Metabolic: Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- blood sugars running in the 110-140 mg/dL range fasting per his report; he is compliant with metformin. No hypoglycemic episodes.
- Psychiatric: Anxiety, mild, related to cancer diagnosis. He is coping with good social support from family.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 136/84 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, temperature 98.4°F, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 83.1 kg (BMI 28.7 kg/m2, stable).

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished gentleman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational and engaged.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae non-injected. Oropharynx without mucositis, ulceration, or thrush. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary chains palpated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 normal. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi. Good air movement.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. Surgical incisions from sigmoid colectomy well-healed, no erythema or dehiscence. Port site on right chest wall without tenderness, swelling, or skin changes -- port accessed without difficulty for today's labs. No palpable abdominal or pelvic masses. Normoactive bowel sounds.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing or cyanosis.

Neurological: Grossly intact cranial nerve examination. Motor strength 5/5 bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. Sensory examination: decreased sensation to light touch in bilateral feet up to mid-calf level; patchy decreased sensation in fingertips bilaterally. Vibration sense diminished in bilateral toes and ankles. Proprioception mildly impaired in bilateral great toes. Deep tendon reflexes diminished at bilateral ankles; present but reduced at bilateral knees and wrists. Gait steady on flat surface; mild widened base noted.

Performance Status: ECOG PS 1.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (today):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.8 x10^3/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 12.4 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, consistent with chronic disease/chemotherapy)
- Platelets: 218 x10^3/uL (normal)
- ANC: 3.2 x10^3/uL (adequate)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride: 102 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 18 mg/dL, Creatinine: 0.94 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
- ALT: 28 U/L, AST: 32 U/L (within normal limits)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 94 U/L (normal)
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (normal)
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL (slightly low normal)
- Glucose: 124 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with known T2DM)

Coagulation: INR 1.1 (normal).

HbA1c (drawn today, result pending -- ordered to reassess glycemic control given ongoing chemotherapy and steroid use with premedications).

Tumor Markers:
- CEA: 35.2 ng/mL (slight downtrend from 38.4 ng/mL at chemotherapy resumption; still significantly above normal but heading in the right direction)
- CA 19-9: 72.4 U/mL (down from 89 U/mL at the cycle-12 scan timepoint)

Urinalysis: No proteinuria (urine protein:creatinine ratio 0.12, within acceptable range for bevacizumab continuation).

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

No new imaging today. Most recent restaging CT (chest/abdomen/pelvis, performed approximately one month prior at the time of the cycle-12 assessment) demonstrated sustained partial response with hepatic lesion burden significantly reduced compared to baseline: dominant segment VII lesion 1.8 cm (down from 3.2 cm at diagnosis), segment IVa 1.1 cm (down from 2.1 cm), segment VI 0.9 cm (down from 1.7 cm), segment II 0.7 cm (down from 1.4 cm). No pulmonary nodules on that scan. No ascites, no peritoneal disease. Anastomotic site without recurrence. A 4-month restaging CT is planned from today's visit to monitor maintenance therapy response.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. Stage IV KRAS G12D-mutant sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases, currently on maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab following completion of 12 cycles of FOLFOX6/5-FU-bevacizumab induction and laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy.
   - Disease status: Stable to responding, with CEA on a slight downtrend (35.2 ng/mL today) and prior imaging demonstrating partial response sustained. Clinical trajectory is favorable at this juncture.
   - Plan: Continue maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab on current dosing without modification. No dose changes indicated today; blood counts are adequate, renal and hepatic function are stable, no proteinuria to warrant bevacizumab dose modification.
   - Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast to be scheduled in approximately 4 months (or sooner if CEA rises or new symptoms develop). Will also repeat CEA and CA 19-9 at next visit in 4 weeks.
   - The patient and I discussed the current status in detail. He understands this is palliative-intent therapy and that hepatic metastases remain present. The residual hepatic disease continues to be followed by hepatobiliary surgery -- a hepatic surgical re-evaluation will be planned around the time of next restaging, particularly to reassess whether any of the remaining lesions might be amenable to ablative or resective approaches given their continued response.

2. Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy, grade 2 (improved from grade 3), with underlying diabetic peripheral neuropathy.
   - The improvement since oxaliplatin discontinuation and gabapentin initiation is encouraging. Current regimen (gabapentin 300 mg TID) is providing partial relief.
   - Continue gabapentin at current dose. Neurology follow-up is in place -- patient to maintain that appointment. If neuropathy plateau persists or worsens, could trial duloxetine as an alternative or adjunct.
   - Occupational therapy referral ongoing to assist with fine motor adaptation strategies.
   - Patient encouraged to report any new falls, increasing imbalance, or worsening of functional status.
   - No oxaliplatin re-challenge planned given severity of prior toxicity and adequate disease control on current backbone.

3. Bevacizumab-associated hypertension on background of essential hypertension.
   - Blood pressure today is 136/84 mmHg, which is at goal. He is on lisinopril 20 mg daily plus amlodipine 5 mg daily (intensified regimen established during induction therapy).
   - Continue current antihypertensive regimen. Home blood pressure monitoring to be maintained; patient instructed to call if readings consistently above 150/95 mmHg.
   - Renal function and urinalysis remain stable, no indication to hold bevacizumab at this time.

4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin.
   - Glucose 124 mg/dL today. HbA1c pending. Metformin continued at current dose.
   - Will review HbA1c result at next visit. If poorly controlled, endocrinology referral can be considered, though steroid premedications with chemotherapy may be contributing to glucose excursions.

5. Mild normocytic anemia, likely multifactorial (chemotherapy effect, prior surgical blood loss, chronic disease).
   - Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, stable. Patient asymptomatic. No transfusion indicated.
   - Iron studies were sent with today's labs -- results pending. Will review at next visit. If iron deficiency identified, can supplement.

6. Goals of care and psychosocial support.
   - Mr. Okafor remains highly engaged and motivated in his treatment. He has adapted well to the functional limitations imposed by neuropathy, has returned to part-time administrative work, and maintains strong family support.
   - Reiterated that current treatment is palliative in intent. Discussed that if the hepatic lesions continue to respond, hepatic-directed therapy options (resection or ablation of residual lesions) will be revisited -- this remains a goal worth pursuing if disease permits.
   - Social work referral maintained from prior visits. Patient has declined additional counseling at this time but was reminded of availability.

RETURN TO CLINIC: 4 weeks for CBC, CMP, CEA, CA 19-9, and cycle assessment. Restaging CT to be scheduled at 4-month mark from today.

Patient verbalized understanding of the above plan and had no additional questions at today's visit.

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist, GI Oncology Service]
